md: A **proportion** is just an equation that says *two ratios are equal*. They show up everywhere — recipes, maps, shadows, scaled drawings — anywhere quantities scale together.
When two ratios are equal, they describe the same relationship. $\dfrac{2}{3} = \dfrac{8}{12}$ — both express 'two parts to three parts'.
If a proportion is true, the **cross-products** are equal:
This is the workhorse trick for solving proportions when one of the four numbers is unknown. Multiply 'across the diagonals' and you get a regular equation you can solve.
### Worked example: solving for an unknown

blocks.title: Why does cross-multiplication work?
blocks.body: Multiply both sides of $\dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{c}{d}$ by $bd$. The left becomes $ad$; the right becomes $bc$. So $ad = bc$. Same equation, different form.

md: **Five pencils cost \$3. How much do 12 pencils cost?**
Set up the proportion. The ratio of pencils to dollars stays the same:
When writing a proportion, **be consistent on both sides**. If pencils are on top of the left fraction, they must also be on top of the right fraction. Mixing them up gives the wrong answer.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: A proportion sets two ratios equal. Solve for an unknown by cross-multiplying ($ad = bc$), then dividing. Set up word problems by keeping like quantities on the same side, top or bottom — never mix them up.
